title: Market failure: public goods and common pool resources
summary: Markets work well for most goods, but two special cases break the price mechanism. Public goods (non-rivalrous and non-excludable) are under-provided because of the free-rider problem, and common pool resources (rivalrous but non-excludable) are over-used, producing the tragedy of the commons. This lesson builds the rivalry/excludability framework, explains both failures through their exact mechanisms, and evaluates the government responses examiners expect — direct provision, regulation, and allocating property rights.
learningObjectives: Classify goods using rivalry and excludability, and distinguish public goods, common pool resources, private goods and club goods.
Explain how non-rivalry and non-excludability create the free-rider problem and cause the market to under-provide public goods.
Explain how rivalry combined with non-excludability causes over-use of common pool resources — the tragedy of the commons — and threatens sustainability.
Evaluate government responses to both failures: direct provision, regulation, and the assignment of property rights.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The Lighthouse and the Overfished Sea
simpleExplanation.summary: Some goods, like a lighthouse beam or street lighting, benefit everyone but are almost impossible to charge for — so a profit-seeking firm won't supply them. Other resources, like fish in the open sea, belong to no one and are free to take — so they get used up too fast. Both are market failures, but for opposite reasons.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine your street wants a security guard to patrol at night. Everyone would feel safer, but there is no way to switch off the safety for anyone who refuses to chip in. So you wait for your neighbour to pay, and they wait for you — and in the end nobody pays and no guard is hired. That is the free-rider problem, and it is why markets under-provide public goods.
steps: Ask two questions about any good: is it RIVALROUS (does my use reduce what's left for you)? Is it EXCLUDABLE (can non-payers be kept out)?
Those two answers sort every good into four boxes: private goods, club goods, common pool resources, and public goods.
Public goods (e.g. national defence) are non-rivalrous AND non-excludable, so the free-rider problem leads to under-provision.
Common pool resources (e.g. ocean fish stocks) are rivalrous BUT non-excludable, so self-interest leads to over-use — the tragedy of the commons.

flashcards.back: A good that is both non-rivalrous and non-excludable, e.g. national defence, street lighting, flood defences. Because non-payers cannot be excluded, a private market under-provides it.
A resource that is rivalrous but non-excludable, e.g. ocean fish stocks, common grazing land, a shared aquifer. Open access plus rivalry leads to over-use.
One person's consumption does not reduce the quantity or quality available to others. One extra person seeing a firework display does not diminish anyone else's view.
It is impossible, or prohibitively costly, to prevent non-payers from consuming the good. You cannot stop a ship benefiting from a lighthouse beam.
One person's consumption prevents another's. If I catch a fish, that fish is no longer available for you to catch.
A consumer can be prevented from using the good unless they pay, e.g. a cinema ticket or a toll gate.

Because a public good is non-excludable, individuals can enjoy it without paying. Everyone has an incentive to let others pay, so willingness to pay is understated, firms cannot cover costs, and the good is under-provided or not provided at all.

When a rivalrous resource is open to all, each user gains the full private benefit of taking more but bears only a fraction of the cost of depletion. Rational self-interest therefore leads to collective over-use and possible collapse of the resource.

flashcards.front: Trap: 'A public good is anything the government provides'
flashcards.back: False. Governments provide many goods that are NOT public goods — state schooling and hospital care are rivalrous and excludable (hence private/merit goods). 'Public good' is a technical label about rivalry and excludability, not about who happens to pay.

flashcards.front: Public good vs common pool resource
flashcards.back: Both are non-excludable, so the difference is RIVALRY. A public good is non-rivalrous (my use doesn't affect yours) → under-provision. A common pool resource is rivalrous (my use depletes yours) → over-use.
Rivalrous and excludable, e.g. a coffee or a smartphone. The vast majority of goods are private goods, and the price mechanism allocates them efficiently.
Excludable but non-rivalrous up to a congestion point, e.g. a streaming subscription, a private golf course, an uncongested toll road. Markets can provide these because non-payers can be excluded.

Legally assigning ownership of a common pool resource — e.g. individual transferable fishing quotas or land title — gives the owner an incentive to conserve it, converting open access into managed access.

quickCheck.prompt: A good is a PUBLIC GOOD (in the economic sense) if and only if it is:
options: Free to the user at the point of use

quickCheck.prompt: State-funded hospital treatment is NOT a public good because it is:
options: Rivalrous and excludable, even though the state provides it
A club good with a congestion point
quickCheck.answer: Rivalrous and excludable, even though the state provides it

quickCheck.prompt: The key characteristic that distinguishes a common pool resource from a public good is that a common pool resource is:
The free market UNDER-provides a public good chiefly because:
options: The good is too expensive to produce
Non-excludability lets consumers free-ride, so firms cannot cover their costs
Consumers do not want the good
The government has banned private provision
quickCheck.answer: Non-excludability lets consumers free-ride, so firms cannot cover their costs

quickCheck.prompt: Ocean fish stocks are OVER-exploited because each fishing boat:
options: Enjoys the full private gain from its catch but bears only part of the cost of depletion
Is legally excluded from the sea
quickCheck.answer: Enjoys the full private gain from its catch but bears only part of the cost of depletion

faq.q: Is the internet a public good?
faq.a: Not strictly. Individual use of a website is largely non-rivalrous (ignoring congestion), but access is excludable — you must pay an Internet Service Provider. Excludability makes it a club (toll) good, not a public good. This is a common exam trap: 'available to many people' is not the same as 'non-excludable'.

faq.q: What is the single difference between a public good and a common pool resource?
faq.a: Rivalry. Both are non-excludable, so anyone can access them. But a public good is non-rivalrous (street lighting — my use doesn't reduce yours), whereas a common pool resource is rivalrous (fish — my catch reduces what's left for you). That one difference flips the failure from UNDER-provision to OVER-use.

faq.q: Why isn't healthcare a public good, even when the government pays for it?
faq.a: In economics 'public good' is a technical label, not a statement about who funds the good. Healthcare is rivalrous (a hospital bed or a doctor's hour used by one patient is unavailable to another) and excludable (providers can and do charge). Governments provide it for equity and positive externalities, which makes it a MERIT good — but it remains, in its characteristics, a private good.

faq.q: If common pool resources are over-used, why not just privatise everything?
faq.a: Assigning property rights (e.g. transferable fishing quotas) can work well and is a genuine solution. But some resources — the deep oceans, the atmosphere — are almost impossible to divide and enforce ownership over, so regulation or international cooperation is needed instead. The right policy depends on how easily rights can be defined and enforced.

sections.content: Welcome to one of the clearest cases of market failure on the whole course. For most goods, the price mechanism does a remarkable job of allocating scarce resources. But some goods are 'shareable' or hard to charge for, and for these the market either produces too little or allows too much to be used up. This lesson examines the two classic examples — public goods and common pool resources — and the government responses that aim to restore efficiency and protect sustainability.
The two building blocks: rivalry and excludability

Every good in economics can be described by two independent properties. Getting these definitions exactly right is the foundation of the whole topic, because both market failures in this lesson are just consequences of particular combinations of the two.

items: **Rivalry**: a good is RIVALROUS if one person's consumption reduces the amount or quality available to others. A slice of pizza is rivalrous; a radio broadcast is non-rivalrous, because my listening does not stop yours.

**Excludability**: a good is EXCLUDABLE if the provider can prevent non-payers from consuming it. A concert ticket makes the show excludable; the reduced crime from police patrols is largely non-excludable, because you cannot switch off the safety for one household on a patrolled street.

sections.content: Classifying goods: the four-box matrix

Combining the two properties sorts all goods into four categories. Learn this matrix — an examiner can test any cell of it, and the two problem cases (public goods and common pool resources) are simply the two non-excludable boxes.

items: **Private goods** — rivalrous AND excludable (a chocolate bar, a car). The market allocates these efficiently.
**Club (toll) goods** — non-rivalrous but excludable (satellite TV, a streaming subscription, an uncongested toll road). Markets can supply these because non-payers can be shut out.
**Common pool resources** — rivalrous but non-excludable (open-sea fish, common grazing land). These are over-used: the tragedy of the commons.
**Public goods** — non-rivalrous AND non-excludable (national defence, street lighting, flood defences). These are under-provided: the free-rider problem.

sections.content: Public goods and the free-rider problem

A pure public good has two features that together defeat the market. Because it is NON-EXCLUDABLE, a firm cannot stop people who refuse to pay from consuming it, so it cannot reliably collect revenue. Because it is NON-RIVALROUS, the efficient price for one more user is actually zero — serving an extra person costs nothing — yet a firm needs a positive price to cover its costs.

Put those together and you get the free-rider problem. Each rational consumer reasons: 'the good will be provided whether or not I pay, and I cannot be excluded, so I will let others pay and consume for free.' When everyone reasons this way, willingness to pay is understated, firms cannot cover their costs, and the good is UNDER-provided or not provided at all — even though the total social benefit exceeds the total cost. This is a market failure, because a good society values is missing purely because of how it must be paid for, not because it isn't worth producing. Note the causal chain examiners look for: non-excludability → free-riding → no revenue → under-provision.

sections.question: Two households, A and B, live on a dark street and could share a single streetlight costing £200. The light is worth £150 to each household. They decide independently whether to contribute £100 each; if only one pays, the light is not installed and the payer loses their £100. What outcome does self-interest produce, and why is it a market failure?
sections.solution:
Because the light is non-excludable (once lit, it shines on both households whether or not they paid) and non-rivalrous (both benefit fully at once), this is a public good, and we can show the free-rider logic with a payoff matrix.

**Payoffs (benefit − contribution):**
- Both contribute → light installed: each gets £150 − £100 = **£50**.
- One contributes, the other doesn't → light NOT installed: the payer loses £100 (**−£100**), the free-rider gets **£0**.
- Neither contributes → no light: **£0** each.

| | B contributes | B doesn't |
|---|---|---|
| **A contributes** | A: £50, B: £50 | A: −£100, B: £0 |
| **A doesn't** | A: £0, B: −£100 | A: £0, B: £0 |

**Reasoning for A:** if B contributes, A prefers not to (£0 > £50? No — but the light still needs BOTH £100 shares, so a lone contributor gets nothing built). Formally: whatever B does, A can never gain by being the only payer, and can avoid a loss by not paying. Not contributing is the dominant strategy — and the same is true for B.

**Outcome:** both free-ride, nobody pays, and the light is not installed.

**Why it is a market failure:** the total social benefit (£150 + £150 = £300) clearly exceeds the total cost (£200), so the socially efficient outcome is to build the light. The market fails to deliver it purely because non-excludability lets each household free-ride. This is exactly why public goods typically require government provision funded by compulsory taxation.

sections.content: Common pool resources and the tragedy of the commons

Common pool resources flip one characteristic. They are still NON-EXCLUDABLE — no one can be kept out — but they are RIVALROUS: every unit consumed is one unit less for everyone else. Open access plus rivalry is a dangerous mix. Each user captures the full private benefit of taking more, but the cost of the resulting depletion is spread across all users. So each individual has a private incentive to take as much as possible, as fast as possible, before someone else does.

When every user follows that logic, the resource is over-exploited and can collapse entirely — the tragedy of the commons. In effect there is a negative externality of consumption: the marginal social cost of using the resource (which includes the depletion imposed on others and on the future) exceeds the marginal private cost each user actually faces. This directly threatens SUSTAINABILITY, because a resource used faster than it can regenerate — a fishery, a forest, an aquifer — cannot support future generations.

sections.question: A lake's fish sell for £10/kg. With 1 boat the catch is 100kg; with 2 boats, 180kg total (90kg each); with 3 boats, 240kg total (80kg each); with 4 boats, 280kg total (70kg each). Each boat costs £750 to operate, and the lake is open to anyone. How many boats will actually fish the lake, and how many SHOULD there be? Explain the divergence.
sections.solution:
Because the lake is non-excludable, anyone may fish; because fish are rivalrous, each extra boat reduces the catch per boat. That is the common pool set-up.

**Private incentive (open access):** a boat enters if its own revenue beats its own cost of £750.
- Boat 1: 100kg × £10 = £1,000 → profit £250. Enters.
- Boat 2: 90kg × £10 = £900 → profit £150. Enters.
- Boat 3: 80kg × £10 = £800 → profit £50. Enters.
- Boat 4: 70kg × £10 = £700 → profit −£50. Stays out.

**Open-access outcome: 3 boats.**

**Social optimum (maximise total profit for the whole fishery):**
- 1 boat: £1,000 − £750 = **£250**
- 2 boats: 180kg × £10 = £1,800 − £1,500 = **£300**
- 3 boats: 240kg × £10 = £2,400 − £2,250 = **£150**

**Social optimum: 2 boats**, where total profit peaks at £300.

**The divergence:** the third boat looks privately worthwhile (£800 > £750), but it is socially wasteful. Adding it raises total revenue only from £1,800 to £2,400 — a gain of £600 — while costing £750 to run, because part of its 'catch' is simply fish taken away from the first two boats. The individual boat ignores that depletion it imposes on others (the external cost), so it enters anyway. Self-interest therefore drives the fishery past its efficient size toward over-exploitation — the tragedy of the commons — and left unchecked the stock is fished down and long-run sustainability is lost.

sections.content: Precision wins marks here. For public goods, always state 'non-rivalrous' AND 'non-excludable', explain each, then drive the causal chain to the FREE-RIDER problem and under-provision. For common pool resources, state 'rivalrous' AND 'non-excludable', then link to the TRAGEDY OF THE COMMONS and over-use. Naming the mechanism — not just the outcome — is what lifts an answer from 'describes' to 'explains'.
Government responses

Because both problems come from the same root — non-excludability that the price mechanism cannot handle — the responses all work by having the state step in where the market cannot. Match the intervention to the failure.

items: **Direct provision (for public goods):** the government supplies the good itself — national defence, street lighting, flood defences — and finances it through compulsory taxation. Taxation defeats the free-rider problem because contribution is no longer voluntary; everyone pays, so the good gets provided.

**Regulation and legislation (mainly for common pool resources):** quotas (e.g. total allowable catch), restricted seasons, gear limits, or outright bans on harmful activity cap the rate of use and give the resource time to regenerate — protecting sustainability.

**Assigning property rights (for common pool resources):** granting ownership — individual transferable fishing quotas, land title over grazing land, or emissions allowances — converts open access into managed access. An owner who bears the future cost of depletion now has an incentive to conserve. This directly targets the non-excludability at the root of the problem.

**Supporting tools:** Pigouvian taxes (raising the private cost of use toward the social cost) and tradable permits / cap-and-trade (a fixed cap with a market price for using the resource) are widely used for pollution and emissions.

**International cooperation:** because oceans and the atmosphere cross borders, no single government can assign rights or regulate alone. Agreements such as the Paris Agreement are needed — though they are hard to negotiate and to enforce.

sections.content: Evaluation matters in Paper 1 too: direct provision requires the government to estimate the right quantity (it lacks the price signals a market would give); quotas need costly monitoring and can be evaded; and property rights can be impossible to define for truly global resources. No single response is a complete fix — the best policy depends on how easily the good's use can be measured, priced and enforced.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Defining a public good as 'anything the government provides'** — the label is about NON-RIVALRY and NON-EXCLUDABILITY, not about who pays. State schooling and hospital care are government-provided but are rivalrous and excludable, so they are merit/private goods, not public goods.

**Confusing public goods with common pool resources** — both are non-excludable, but public goods are NON-rivalrous (→ under-provision), whereas common pool resources are RIVALROUS (→ over-use). Getting rivalry the wrong way round reverses the entire analysis.

**Naming the free-rider problem without explaining the mechanism** — you must show the chain: non-excludability → consumers free-ride → firms cannot collect revenue → under-provision. 'People don't pay' on its own is not the mechanism.
**Saying the market UNDER-provides common pool resources** — no: it OVER-uses them. Under-provision is the public-good failure; over-consumption is the common-pool failure.

**Treating a club good (e.g. cable TV, a toll road) as a public good** — it is non-rivalrous but EXCLUDABLE, so the market can and does supply it. Non-excludability is the essential ingredient of a public good.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (a): Explain why public goods are not provided by a free market, with reference to the characteristics of non-rivalry and non-excludability. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
A public good is defined by two characteristics: it is NON-RIVALROUS — one person's consumption does not reduce the amount available to others (e.g. one more ship using a lighthouse beam does not dim it for other ships) — and NON-EXCLUDABLE — it is impossible, or prohibitively costly, to prevent non-payers from consuming it. Examples include national defence, street lighting and flood defences.

These two characteristics together explain why a free market fails to provide the good. Because the good is non-excludable, a private firm cannot prevent people who refuse to pay from consuming it, so it cannot reliably charge a price or collect revenue. This creates the FREE-RIDER PROBLEM: each rational consumer knows they cannot be excluded, so they have an incentive to let others pay and then consume for free. When every consumer reasons this way, willingness to pay is systematically understated and the firm cannot cover its costs.

Non-rivalry compounds the problem. Since serving one extra consumer costs the firm nothing, the efficient price is zero — yet at a price of zero no profit-seeking firm will supply the good at all. The result is that the free market UNDER-provides the public good, or does not provide it at all, even though the total social benefit exceeds the total cost of provision. This is a market failure, because society would gain from the good but the price mechanism cannot deliver it. It is precisely why such goods are typically financed through compulsory taxation and provided directly by the government.

*(A diagram is optional for this question — 'where appropriate' — because the failure is fully explained through the free-rider mechanism in words; a figure is not essential to reach the top band here.)*

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (a), out of 10):**
- **9–10:** demands addressed, theory FULLY explained, terms THROUGHOUT, where appropriate diagram included and FULLY explained.
- **7–8:** theory explained, terms mostly appropriate.
- **5–6:** partially addressed, theory PARTLY explained, some terms.
- **3–4:** theory DESCRIBED, some terms.
- **1–2:** not relevant.

Why the model answer reaches **9–10**:
- ✅ **Demands addressed:** it directly answers WHY the free market does not provide the good, explicitly referencing both non-rivalry and non-excludability as the question requires.
- ✅ **Theory FULLY explained:** it does not merely name the free-rider problem — it derives it, tracing the full causal chain (non-excludability → free-riding → no revenue → under-provision) and adds the non-rivalry/zero-price argument.
- ✅ **Terms used THROUGHOUT:** non-rivalrous, non-excludable, free-rider problem, under-provision, market failure and social benefit are deployed accurately across the whole answer, not just defined once.
- ✅ **Diagram 'where appropriate':** here a diagram is NOT essential, so its absence does not cap the mark. The band asks for a diagram only 'where appropriate', and for this particular question the mechanism is fully explained in prose — so the response still qualifies for the top band. (An answer that only DESCRIBED the terms without the free-rider mechanism would fall to the 3–4 band; one that partly explained it would sit at 5–6.)

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official markband — telling you which band you reached and why. The habit that moves you up the bands here: don't just NAME the free-rider problem — EXPLAIN the chain from non-excludability to under-provision.
Key concepts in this lesson

This lesson develops three of the course's nine key concepts. **Efficiency** is lost in both cases — public goods are under-provided and common pool resources over-used relative to the social optimum. **Sustainability** is central to common pool resources, where over-use can deplete a fishery, forest or aquifer beyond recovery. And **intervention** is the response: direct provision, regulation and property rights are all ways the government steps in where the price mechanism cannot. Linking a key concept to real-world material is specifically rewarded in the internal assessment, so keep these three in view.

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (a) answer marked: explain why the free market does not provide public goods
